coredata.dc:title: Enhancing capacitance performance of functional group assisted carbon quantum dots derived from turmeric plant waste
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Patil, S.S.
Bhosale, A.G.
Kundale, S.S.
Dongale, T.D.
Vanalakar, S.A.
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors have attracted significant attention in modern devices as a promising solution for electrical energy storage due to their remarkable capability to undergo rapid charge and discharge cycles. While various materials are employed in the construction of supercapacitors, carbon-based materials emerge as a predominant choice within the commercial realm. In present report, our intention is to develop an effective supercapacitor device derived from natural biomass. Therefore, we have synthesized water soluble, monodisperse and fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from turmeric leaves (Curcuma caesia) via a single step hydrothermal carbonization. Further, the doctor blade technique was employed to coat a layer of CQDs on stainless steel substrate using PVA as a binder. We observed the functional groups associated with QDs triggers the fast diffusion of ions and transmission of electrons with conducting substrate and electrolyte and thereby effectively charge and discharge mechanism. The supercapacitor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based electrode exhibits exceptional performance characteristics with a remarkable specific capacitance of 468 F/g and highest energy density of 78.6 Wh/kg, superior to the values reported for most carbon-based supercapacitors. Further, we demonstrated the light dependent capacitive enhancement by depositing a thin P3HT layer over CQDs. Moreover, CQDs-based supercapacitor achieves a maximum power density of 733.2 W/kg when operated in a 1 M KOH electrolyte solution and an excellent capacitive retention of about 80 % even after 5000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Turmeric leaves
Functional group
Supercapacitor
Photocapacitor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Author SSP would like thankful the Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune (Government of Maharashtra) for the financial support under the Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj National Research Fellowship-(CSMNRF-2021) and Department of Physics, K.R.P Kanya Mahavidyala Islampur, Shivaji University Kolhapur, M.S. India for providing research facilities. This project received funding from Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (Project no. 121C046).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of preparation of turmeric based CQDs by hydrothermal method along with the photograph of CQDs under normal and UV light.
The physicochemical properties of CQDs including (a) Raman spectrum, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) XPS spectrum, and (d) the particle size distribution. The inset shows the TEM image of CQDs.

The electrochemical properties of CQDs based electrode including (a) CV for 10 mV/s, (b) variation of specific capacitance with respect to scan rate, (c) effect of visible light on CV for 10 mV/s, (d) CV of CQDs-P3HT under blue light and red-light illumination.

The Galvanostatic charge-discharge of CQDs based electrode including (a) GCD for the current density of 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 mA/cm2, (b) variation of specific capacitance with current density, (c) comparison of specific capacitances of previously reported CQDs-based electrodes with present report and (d) performance of parallel combination of CQDs electrodes for 0.6 mA/cm2.

Electrochemical impedance and stability of CQDs based supercapacitor including (a) EIS and (b) a graph of capacitance retention and Coulombic efficiency as a function of cycles showing about 80 % retention even after 5000 cycles. Inset shows the GCD of 1st and 5000th cycle.

The process of fabrication of CQDs based supercapacitor including, (a) photographs of positive and negative electrodes made up with CQDs, (b) schematic presentation of supercapacitor device showing the CQDs based cathode, anode alongwith the separator, (c) photograph of CQDs based supercapacitor device and (d) the glowing of LED by CQDs based supercapacitor device.

The performance of symmetrical CQDs based supercapacitor device including, (a) CV curves at different scan rates (10 mV/s to 100 mV/s), (b) GCD curves at a current density varying from 0.4 mA/cm2 to 0.8 mA/cm2, (c) Nyquist plots recorded for the CQDs device and (d) variation of energy density with respect to power density.

head.ce:title: Enhancing capacitance performance of functional group assisted carbon quantum dots derived from turmeric plant waste

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Smt. Kusumtai Rajarambapu Patil Kanya Mahavidyalaya, Islampur, (Affiliated to Shivaji University, Kolhapur), 415409, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Karmaveer Hire Arts, Science, Commerce and Education College, Gargoti, (Affiliated to Shivaji University, Kolhapur) 416209, MS, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Science, Commerce and Education College, Gargoti

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, Karmaveer Hire Arts, Science, Commerce and Education College, Gargoti, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416209, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Biotechnology, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Nanoscience and Biotechnology, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004, MS, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors have attracted significant attention in modern devices as a promising solution for electrical energy storage due to their remarkable capability to undergo rapid charge and discharge cycles. While various materials are employed in the construction of supercapacitors, carbon-based materials emerge as a predominant choice within the commercial realm. In present report, our intention is to develop an effective supercapacitor device derived from natural biomass. Therefore, we have synthesized water soluble, monodisperse and fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from turmeric leaves (Curcuma caesia) via a single step hydrothermal carbonization. Further, the doctor blade technique was employed to coat a layer of CQDs on stainless steel substrate using PVA as a binder. We observed the functional groups associated with QDs triggers the fast diffusion of ions and transmission of electrons with conducting substrate and electrolyte and thereby effectively charge and discharge mechanism. The supercapacitor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based electrode exhibits exceptional performance characteristics with a remarkable specific capacitance of 468 F/g and highest energy density of 78.6 Wh/kg, superior to the values reported for most carbon-based supercapacitors. Further, we demonstrated the light dependent capacitive enhancement by depositing a thin P3HT layer over CQDs. Moreover, CQDs-based supercapacitor achieves a maximum power density of 733.2 W/kg when operated in a 1 M KOH electrolyte solution and an excellent capacitive retention of about 80 % even after 5000 cycles.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The generation of electrical energy is a fundamental and ever-growing requirement that underpins nearly every aspect of modern life. In the current context, efficient energy storage devices are crucial alongside energy sources. For instance, the transition to electric vehicles in the transportation industry necessitates efficient energy storage systems to enable longer driving range and shorter charging times. In the quest for sustainable energy storage, supercapacitors have emerged as a revolutionary technology with the potential to reshape the energy storage landscape offering inimitable advantages such as high power density, rapid charge/discharge rates, reliability and long cycle life [1]. These remarkable capabilities have ignited interest across various industries and research domains, positioning supercapacitors as a critical component in achieving a cleaner, more efficient, and sustainable future [2]. The hierarchical structure of electrode materials also helps to enhance electrochemical performance [3,4]. If the materials utilized to fabricate the supercapacitors are environmentally friendly, biological originated and biodegradable then it aligns with the broader goal of achieving sustainable development, minimizing environmental impact, and transitioning towards a more circular economy.

In this direction, the development of biologically synthesized materials for the fabrication of supercapacitor is an exciting and promising path in energy storage research [5,6]. With biological systems, researchers are investigating new ways to develop high-performing, environmentally friendly, and sustainable materials for supercapacitors. Using carbon-based compounds made from biomass or waste materials through crude processes like hydrothermal carbonization or pyrolysis is one well-known example [7]. These carbon materials can exhibit high surface area, excellent conductivity, and tailored porosity, making them well-suited for electrode materials in supercapacitors [7]. Meanwhile, among the carbon-based family, a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been attracting increasing attention due to their unique idiosyncratic properties such as biocompatibility, aqueous and electrochemical stability, high surface area and electrical conductivity, and low cost [8].

The biological materials such as fruit and plant extract, microorganisms, algae and cyanobacteria, biomass and waste materials like agriculture, food waste or rice husk have been utilized for the extraction and synthesis of CQDs [9,10]. Among them, the plant extracts can provide diverse precursor molecules and functional groups, resulting in CQDs with varying properties. For example, green tea extracts contain polyphenols that contribute to the synthesis of CQDs with antioxidant and photostable properties [11]. Meanwhile, the functional groups, such as phenols, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups, play important roles in supercapacitor applications [12]. For instance carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), and amino (NH2) groups can create more active sites for electrolyte ions to adsorb [13,14]. The functional groups like carboxyl (COOH) and sulfonic acid (SO3H) can improve the adhesion between active material particles and the current collector and reducing electrode degradation [15-17]. Table S1 shows the details of functional groups associated with plant leaves extracted CQDs. The functional groups provide opportunities for tailoring the surface properties, conductivity, redox behavior, and stability of electrode materials in supercapacitors [18]. By carefully selecting and engineering these functional groups, it is possible to enhance the overall performance and properties of supercapacitor electrodes, leading to improved energy storage capabilities. Unfortunately, there is an inadequate exposure for CQDs associated with functional groups in supercapacitor device.

In the present study, we decided to use turmeric leaves to extract the CQDs, as the turmeric leaves contain various functional groups, such as phenols, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups [19]. These functional groups can be transferred to CQDs during the extraction process, resulting in a surface rich in functional moieties. The functional group assisted CQDs offer the fast electron transfer, flexibility and high performance of the supercapacitor. In addition, it facilitates further chemical modifications and surface functionalization of CQDs, enhancing their stability, dispersibility, and compatibility with specific applications. Moreover, the turmeric leaves were selected to extract the CQDs, owing to their sustainable and low-cost synthesis, biocompatibility and non-toxicity, tunable optical properties along with the abundance of functional groups. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first report of using carbon quantum dots extracted from turmeric leaves for charge storage devices. The work reveals the successful utilization of the functional groups associated with these CQDs. During study, we succeed to assemble the CQDs based electrodes in parallel arrangement to increase the capacitance at high current density. During this study, we have demonstrated visible light assisted enhancement in the capacitance by combining CQDs with P3HT for the first time. Finally, comparing the CQD-based electrode to previous results in the literature, a higher specific capacitance is observed.

The CQDs were prepared using turmeric leaves (Curcuma caesia) as a raw material in a simplistic hydrothermal unit. In a typical experiment, the turmeric leaves were cleaned with acetone after being rinsed around four times with distilled water. They were then exposed to the sunlight for 5–6 days to dry. Using a mortar and pestle, the dried turmeric leaves were pounded into a powder. The turmeric powder was then dissolved in 100 cc of distilled water, and the mixture was then ultrasonically processed for 30 min at room temperature. A 10 ml of 1 M KOH was added to the mixture for the promotion of carbonization. The mixture was then put into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon vessel that had a 150 cc capacity. The autoclave was heated to 200 °C and kept for 4 h and then allowed to cool down naturally. After the reaction, the dark brown solution was centrifuged for four times at 12,000 rpm to remove impurities and unreacted materials. The liquid supernatant that resulted had a brilliant yellow color, and further any contaminants were removed using filter paper. A stock solution was then made by dispersing the CQDs in the resultant solution.

The structural features and particle sizes were analyzed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) FEI TALOS F200S TEM 200 kV. The surface chemical composition was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) conducted with a Bruker Tiger S8 X-ray fluorescence spectrometer. Micro-Raman spectrometry was performed with a Renishaw model (Renishaw INVIA–Raman Microscope) to provide insights into the material's properties. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific iS10 FT-IR) was employed to identify functional groups in the synthesized samples. The optical properties were assessed through UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) measurements, utilizing Shimadzu UV-3600 Spectrophotometer. For electrochemical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), stability assessment, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), thin films were evaluated within a three-electrode system. A potentiostat (PGSTAT302N Metrohm Autolab, Netherlands) was employed for these measurements. The amount of active material deposited was determined by the weight difference method, comparing the substrate's weight before and after deposition.

For the construction of a working electrode, the mixture of CQDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was prepared in an aqueous solution in the ratio 9:1. The PVA binder was included to ensure the attachment of the active material to the electrode during the production process. This method of blending creates a uniform mixture that can be applied thinly using the doctor blade approach on stainless steel substrates measuring 1 cm by 1 cm. The coated substrates were then dried for two hours at 200 °C in an oven. Using a Whatman filter paper separator, the optimized coated substrate served as an electrode in the construction of the supercapacitor device. Before being enclosed in a pouch, the PVA-KOH gel, which served as the electrolyte, was injected in between the two electrodes. The silver paste was used for the electrical contacts.

Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were performed on the supercapacitor device. These tests evaluated several performance characteristics of the supercapacitor, including its cycle lifetime, responsiveness across various potential regions, current contributions, and the behavior of the symmetrical device (CQDs//CQDs). A 1 M KOH electrolyte was used for all measurements, which included potential ranges from 0.2 V to −0.9 V.

The CQDs were prepared using turmeric leaves via simplistic hydrothermal route and the product is showing olive green color under UV light illumination. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of the preparation of CQDs using turmeric leaves along with their quantum size effect. The prepared CQDs were analyzed using various techniques as shown in Fig. 2 . The Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 2(a)) shows two prominent peaks located at 1306 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 associated with the G and D mode, respectively. The intense G mode confirm the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms which is beneficial for supercapacitor application due to high surface area [20] and conductivity owing to their pi bonds and delocalized electrons.

The IR, NMR and XPS spectroscopy were used to analyze the presence of functional groups associated with turmeric leave derived CQDs. Fig. 2(b) shows the IR spectrum of CQDs containing multiple peaks. The major peak located at 1086 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of CO bonds. The stretching vibration of OH bonds in hydroxyl functional groups is commonly linked to the peak at 3300 cm−1. The existence of saturated hydrocarbons in the CQDs is indicated by the peak at 2960 cm−1, which is related to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds in alkanes. In conjugated systems, the stretching vibrations of CO/CC bonds are frequently linked to the peak at 1600 cm−1. It indicates the existence of double bonds, which are typical of alkenes and aromatic compounds. Alkanes' CH bond bending vibrations are typically responsible for the peak at 1402 cm−1 and is related to scissoring vibrations when CH bonds are present in aliphatic hydrocarbons. The signal at 1113 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibrations of CO bonds, which are frequent in compounds containing carbonyl groups [21]. To obtain additional structural information of CQDs, we used NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S1(a)) (see Supplementary Information). The three prominent peaks located at 1.61, 4.83 and 7.26 ppm were associated with protons in alkylene group, ethers and other aromatic compounds [22]. The chemical shift at about 1.6 ppm is showing protons on carbon chain-like structure. In our study, the protons are also in electronegative atoms region which suggest the presence of functional groups (4.8 ppm). By using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the attachment of functional groups to the CQD surface was further investigated. The presence of various bonding environments of carbon atoms in the sample is indicated by a superposition of three peaks in the XPS spectrum of CQDs in the energy range of 283–291 eV (Fig. 2(c)). The CC bond causes the first binding-energy peak (284.8 eV), whereas other two peaks located at 286.5 eV and 289.2 eV are due to COH and COOH or CO bonds, respectively [23].

In connection with IR, NMR and XPS analysis, we have recorded the optical absorption and photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs and were presented in Fig. S1(b) (see Supplementary Information). The absorption peak of CQDs lies at about 250 nm is attributed to π−π* transition of the CC bond and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum suggest the maximum peak intensity of CQDs in the range of blue-green color. The TEM images reveal the spherical shape of CQDs with a size in the range of 8 to 11 nm by counting 35 particles. (Figs. 2(d) and S1(c) (see Supplementary Information)).

The cyclic voltammetry (CV), Galvanic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) methods have been used to study the electrochemical performances of CQDs in three electrode systems with Graphite as a counter and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode in 1 M KOH electrolyte at a room temperature. Fig. 3 (a) revealed that the CQDs functions via an electric double-layer energy storage mechanism based on electrolyte ion adsorption/desorption on the electrode surface, as shown by the lack of any redox peaks from a faradaic process [24]. Meanwhile, the formulas used to calculate electrochemical parameters such as specific capacitance, energy and power densities, quantification of diffusive and capacitive contribution and Columbic efficiency were presented in Supplementary Information (Eqs. (1S) to (6S)) (see Supplementary Information). Fig. 3(b) shows the variation of the capacitance of CQDs with respect to the scan rate, and maximum specific capacitance of about 140 F/g were observed under the scan rate of 10 mV/s. The higher capacitance mechanism relies on the presence of functional group of CQDs [25]. Irrespective of the scan rate, the shape of CV plots remains similar with no indication of the redox peaks, confirming non-Faradaic charge transfer process. However, the capacitance decreases with the increment in the scan rate due to quick transfer of ions over a short time. Meanwhile, in order to study the contribution of capacitive and Faradaic reactions in the CQDs based supercapacitor, we have analyzed CV curves with the help of Eq. (1S), and the outcome were presented in Fig. S2 (see Supplementary Information). The performance of CQDs is dominated by capacitive reactions irrespective of the scan rate. We further investigated the effect of visible light such as blue (λ = 450 nm) and red (λ = 625 nm) on the capacitive performance of CQDs using LEDs having intensity of 50 mW/cm2 (Fig. 3(c)). However, due the lack of absorbance in the visible spectrum, there is no significant change in the capacitance under light, but interestingly the orientation of CV gets changed.

To study the effect of light illumination on the specific capacitance, we have spin coated P3HT over the CQDs and light of different wavelength were incident on the assembly in three-electrode system. Meanwhile, the protocol reported in the literature were used to prepare and deposit P3HT over CQDs/stainless steel substrate [26]. In short, P3HT (Sigma-Aldrich) and dichlorobenzene were mixed to prepare the P3HT solution and then it is spin coated at 3000 rpm on top of the CQDs film. In the presence of light, the specific capacitance demonstrated an increase, rising to 160 F/g when exposed to red light illumination. This phenomenon is seen in materials having photoactive or photovoltaic characteristics. The enhancement in the specific capacitance under light is attributed to the generation of more charge carriers within a substance. These charge carriers add to the overall charge storage capacity, resulting in an increase in specific capacitance. Such a light dependent capacitance enhancement ability of semiconductors have wide applicability in portable and wearable electronic devices [27].

We performed Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD) tests at various current densities to investigate the stability, voltage drop, and energy storage/release characteristics of the CQDs-based electrode. Fig. 4 (a) shows the GCD curve of CQDs as a function of current densities. As like CV graphs (Fig. 3b), GCD curve (Fig. 4a) reveals an analogous isosceles triangle-like shape irrespective of the current densities, however, the charging and discharging time varies drastically. The higher discharge time with Columbic efficiency (η) at current density of 0.4 mA/cm2 confirms CQDs as a superior electrode material. Meanwhile, Columbic efficiency (η) has been determined through the ratio of charging and discharging times using Eq. (2S) (see Supplementary Information). According to Eq. (S3), the specific capacitance was calculated, and the values were presented in Fig. 4(b). The CQDs based electrode exhibits a remarkable specific capacitance of 468 F/g and is higher than the previous report [25,28-34]. Fig. 4(c) indicates that our CQD-based electrode outperforms at current density in the range 0.5 to 1 mA/cm2 than most previous findings on CQDs or composite electrodes in supercapacitor measurements [30,35-39].

Meanwhile, we observe the reduction in the specific capacitance with the rise in the current density might be due to the polarization effect. The constrained charging and discharging owing to the diffusion of ions within the CQDs based electrode at high current density leads to the lower capacitance. Although, this trend is not a significant concern for power applications, but for energy applications, it is not desirable. Therefore, to enhance the charging discharging properties of CQDs at higher current density, we have assembled two CQDs based capacitors in parallel. We observe the enhancement (284 F/g owing to parallel combination as compared to 156 F/g for single capacitor for 0.6 mA/cm2) in the specific capacitance due to the parallel combination of the capacitors in given potential window (Fig. 4(d)). In the meantime, the different parameters of as synthesized CQDs based capacitors is presented in Table (S2). In the context of supercapacitors, the energy density and power density are two crucial performance parameters, therefore, we have calculated their values using Eqs. (4S) and (5S). In our CQDs based supercapacitor, the energy and power densities were found about 78.6 Wh/kg and 733.24 W/kg, respectively.

The CV and GCD study reveal the working capability of turmeric leaf extracted CQDs. Nevertheless, in order to investigate the mechanism influencing the capacitance and confirm the stated composition (0.3 mg/cm2 of CQDs), we have prepared two additional electrodes with mass loadings of 0.6 mg/cm2 and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs. Therefore, the active mass of CQDs for these sample are about 0.3, 0.6 and 0.9 mg/cm2. Meanwhile, the scanning electrode microscope (SEM) is used to study the surface morphology and thickness of these three samples. Fig. S3 (see Supplementary Information) shows the surface morphology, thickness, and elemental analysis of CQDs samples prepared with 0.3, 0.6 and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs powder. All films are fully covered by the CQDs and does not show any voids and defects. The thickness of three electrodes made with mass loadings of 0.3, 0.6, and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs were found about 25, 45 and 75 μm, respectively. The EDX analysis (Fig. S3(g–i)) confirm the presence of carbon in the films. As the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs is important to the practical application and is related to charge transfer, therefore, we have measured the QY of all CQDs samples. The variation in the quantum yield among these three CQDs samples (QY of 12, 4 and 2 % for samples prepared with 0.3, 0.6 and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs) could indicate the difference in their charge transfer and electrochemical properties. A higher quantum yield might correspond to more efficient charge transfer. Therefore, the thin film prepared with 0.3 mg/cm2 of CQDs is outperforming among its counterparts.

Further, we studied the EIS of all CQDs samples using same experimental set up (Fig. 5 (a)). Except sample prepared with 0.3 mg/cm2 of CQDs, other samples show the significant Warburg resistance (W), which is the indication of diffusion process at electrode-electrolyte interface (Fig. S4(a)) (see Supplementary Information). Further CV of all above-mentioned samples were measured and presented in Fig. S4(b and c) (see Supplementary Information). The CV graph shows minor oxidation–reduction peaks at 0 and −0.9 volts for the samples prepared with 0.6 and 0.9 mg of CQDs, however, the sample prepared with 0.3 mg of CQDs does not reveals significant redox pair. In addition, the specific capacitance reducing with the increase in CQDs amount on the substrate (Fig. S4(d) (see Supplementary Information)) and the trend is like report available in literature [40]. Further, we calculated the equivalent solution resistance (Rs) and charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and thereby an equivalent circuit was drawn using ZSimpWIN 3.21 software for all CQDs samples (0.3, 0.6 and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs) and were presented in Fig. S5(a–c) (see Supplementary Information). The value of Rs and Rct of CQDs electrode is about 2 and 500 Ω, respectively for the 0.3 mg/cm2 of CQDs loading and suggesting the faster charge-discharge rate and improved charge transfer nature of CQDs and thereby the capacitive performance. The value of Rs and Rct for other samples such as 0.6 mg/cm2 of CQDs and 0.9 mg/cm2 of CQDs were found 1.2 and 200 Ω and 1.8 and 140 Ω, respectively. However, with the increment in the mass loading there is noticeable presence of Warburg resistance. We belief that the functional groups assisted with carbon and the proper fabrication of electrode harmonize results into better specific capacitance.

The cyclic stability of the as-synthesized CQDs electrode were tested for about 5000 cycles and we observed its fast charging and discharging ability without degradation (Fig. 5(b)). Even after 5000 charging-discharging cycles, the capacitance retention was not lower than 80 %, affirming the robustness of CQDs electrode. Interestingly, the shape of GCD improves with the enhancement in the charge-discharge cycle (inset of Fig. 5(b)) due to the availability of the reaction cites.

We opted to build the supercapacitor device on a flexible stainless-steel substrate utilizing optimized CQDs samples. Because the operational voltage window of CQDs typically extends into both positive and negative regions (Fig. 3(a)), therefore, we fabricated a symmetric supercapacitor device having CQDs with 0.3 mg/cm2 mass loading on both electrodes. To make the semi-solid gel, 3 g of PVA (polyvinyl acetate) were dissolved in 40 ml DDW at a temperature of 80 °C and the solution was constantly agitated to form a translucent gel-like solution. The PVA + KOH gel electrolyte was then painted over the CQDs-based electrodes, and the assembly was allowed to dry at room temperature. The Whatman filter paper was employed as a separator and to avoid any short through the side of the electrodes, the assembly was sealed with insulating tape. In addition, the assembly was subjected to a hydraulic pressure of 1 tonne for 30 min in order to construct a solid-state symmetric supercapacitor. Fig. 6 shows the steps involving in the fabrication of symmetric supercapacitor device.

The charge storage mechanism at the interface of electrode-electrolyte in symmetric supercapacitor device is shown in Fig. 6(b). It is mostly caused by the electrostatic interaction. In present case, the electric double layer (EDL) is formed at electrode-electrolyte interface of both positive and negative CQDs based electrodes, owing to the adsorption of K+ and OH− ions. During the charging cycles, the ions are drawn to the electrode surface while it is released back into the electrolyte during discharging cycle, facilitating rapid and effective energy storage. Following reactions shows overall mechanism during movement of ions in EDL within charge-discharge cycles (Fig. 6(c)).

During charging (Adsorption)
(1) C + KOH → C− + K+ + e− (at both electrodes)

During discharging (Desorption)
(2) C− + K+ + e − → C (at both electrodes)

Fig. 7 (a) shows the CV at various scan rate of the CQDs based supercapacitor device. Here also the redox peaks are absent suggesting the balanced charge storage between the electrodes [41]. Furthermore, the GCD measurements were carried out at various current densities, as presented in Fig. 7(b). For the 0.4 mA/cm2 current density, a maximum capacitance of 106 F/g was observed. Further, to examine the rate dynamics the EIS analysis is conducted, and the respective Nyquist plot is presented in Fig. 7(c). Tables S3 and S4 shows the EIS parameters using equivalent circuit for turmeric based CQDs electrode supercapacitor devices. The values of the solution resistance and the charge transfer resistance are quite similar with the values found for Fig. 5(a), suggesting the good performance of the device. Meanwhile, an equivalent circuit of device were presented in Fig. S6 (see Supplementary Information). Further, an energy density and power density of the CQDs based symmetric supercapacitor device was calculated and presented in Fig. 7(d). The moderate energy density of about 15 Wh kg−1 and a high-power density of around 200 W kg−1 suggest ability of device to deliver energy quickly than attaining capacity of extremely high energy storage.

We have demonstrated a green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using turmeric leaves by simplistic hydrothermal technique. We observed the functional group attachment with the CQDs owing to the inherent properties of turmeric leaves. CQDs with uniform sizes ranging from 8 to 11 nm in diameter were prepared, and electrodes were made using the doctor blade method. The effect of variation of the mass load, scan rate, current density, and light wavelength on the capacitance of a CQDs-based electrode was investigated. At 0.4 mA/cm2, the as-synthesized CQDs electrode material exhibits astounding electrocapacitive performance, with the maximum specific capacitance of 467.87 F/g. Furthermore, after 5000 cycles, the as-synthesized CQDs displayed exceptional capacitive retention, suggesting their electrocapacitive nature. A supercapacitor device was also built using CQDs-based positive and negative electrodes and a PVA-assisted KOH electrolyte. The device has a high energy density of about 78.62 Wh/kg and a reasonably good power density of 733.24 W/kg, suggesting that it has a great potential for practical applications in energy storage devices.
Not applicable.
S.S. Patil: Conceptualization. A.G. Bhosale: Supervision. S.S. Kundale: Software. T.D. Dongale: Software. S.A. Vanalakar: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author SSP would like thankful the Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune (Government of Maharashtra) for the financial support under the Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj National Research Fellowship-(CSMNRF-2021) and Department of Physics, K.R.P Kanya Mahavidyala Islampur, Shivaji University Kolhapur, M.S. India for providing research facilities. This project received funding from Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (Project no. 121C046).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2024.100370.
